title: Natural Join
content:
### Natural Join

NATURAL JOIN automatically joins tables based on columns with the same name and compatible data types in both tables.

---

### NATURAL JOIN Syntax

```sql
SELECT columns
FROM table1
NATURAL JOIN table2;
```

### NATURAL JOIN Example

If both tables have a column named `dept_id`:

```sql
-- Automatically joins on dept_id
SELECT e.name, d.dept_name
FROM employees e
NATURAL JOIN departments d;

-- Equivalent to (if the only common column is dept_id):
SELECT e.name, d.dept_name
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id;
```

### NATURAL JOIN vs INNER JOIN

Both use INNER JOIN logic, but NATURAL JOIN automatically detects the join columns:

| Aspect | NATURAL JOIN | Explicit JOIN |
|--------|-------------|---------------|
| Join condition | Automatic | Manual ON clause |
| Column matching | Same name columns | Any specified condition |
| Control | Less control | Full control |
| Readability | Concise | Explicit |
| Maintenance risk | Schema changes may break silently | Clear what is joined |

### Risks of NATURAL JOIN

```sql
-- If both tables have 'name' and 'id' columns:
SELECT *
FROM employees
NATURAL JOIN departments;
-- Joins on ALL common columns: (id, name, dept_id) maybe unintended
```

### USING Clause (Alternative)

The USING clause provides more control while being concise:

```sql
-- Only join on dept_id, even if other columns match
SELECT e.name, d.dept_name
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d USING (dept_id);
```

> **Key Point:** NATURAL JOIN is rarely used in production because it can produce unexpected results when table schemas change. Explicit JOIN with ON is preferred for clarity and maintainability.
codeExample:
-- This automatically joins on common column names
SELECT *
FROM orders
NATURAL JOIN customers;

quiz.question: How does NATURAL JOIN determine the join condition?
options: By matching primary and foreign keys
By joining on columns with the same name
By the first column in each table

quiz.question: Why is NATURAL JOIN often avoided in production?
options: It can cause unexpected results when schemas change
